pattern: Because the number of equal-value pairs inside a window only grows as elements are added and only shrinks as they are removed, a two-pointer sweep can find, for every right endpoint, the leftmost start that still reaches k pairs -- and every start further left than that is automatically valid too.
hints: For a fixed right endpoint, growing the window by moving its left edge outward never removes a pair; only shrinking the window (moving the left edge inward) can drop the pair count -- pair count behaves monotonically with window size.

Keep a running tally of equal-value pairs as you extend the window one element at a time on the right, and greedily shrink from the left whenever the window still holds at least k pairs after dropping its leftmost element.

Once you've shrunk as far as possible while staying at or above k pairs, every start index from 0 up to (but not including) your current left pointer also produces a valid subarray ending at this right endpoint -- so the left pointer's current value can be added straight to the running total.

edge_inputs.description: every element identical, threshold reachable by short windows
all distinct values, no pair ever forms
threshold far above the most pairs any subarray of this array could ever hold
single element, so no pair of indices can exist at all
exactly one repeated pair separated by an unrelated value
two interleaved values that alternate, so pairs only accumulate once the window is wide enough
a short run of duplicates followed by a longer run of a different duplicate
two elements at the maximum allowed value, minimal threshold

stress.description: large array drawn from a small value alphabet so duplicate pairs are common, threshold scaled to the array size
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    alphabet = max(1, min(50, n))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, alphabet) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, max(1, n))
    return [nums, k]

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def countGood(self, nums, k):
        freq = {}
        left = 0
        pairs = 0
        ans = 0
        for right in range(len(nums)):
            x = nums[right]
            pairs += freq.get(x, 0)
            freq[x] = freq.get(x, 0) + 1
            while pairs >= k:
                y = nums[left]
                pairs -= freq[y] - 1
                freq[y] -= 1
                left += 1
            ans += left
        return ans
